user_prompt: shine encoder may not be respecting the disable_bit_reservoir option
do not edit code, simply check this, it seems to work, or maybe I lose one packet only if locally playing back while scrubbing?
agent_reply: I checked it read-only. Short version: your suspicion is half right, but probably not the cause of the observed behavior.

`disable_bit_reservoir` is accepted by [ShineWrapper.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/ShineWrapper.cpp:17) and stored as `disable_bit_reservoir_`, but I found no code that actually uses that member. So as an option, it is not respected.
But the vendored Shine encoder appears to have the reservoir effectively disabled already:
- [reservoir.c](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/shine/src/lib/reservoir.c:30) immediately returns normal per-frame bits when `ResvMax` is zero.
- [l3bitstream.c](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/shine/src/lib/l3bitstream.c:103) writes `main_data_begin` as zero for both MPEG-I and MPEG-II paths.
So the flag is a no-op, but the actual output should still be no-reservoir. Since your recorder is 16 kHz / 64 kbps MPEG-II Layer III, frames should be standalone enough for frame-aligned scrubbing.

The “lose one packet while locally playing back while scrubbing” sounds more like playback mechanics than encoder reservoir. `ScrubBar` calls the scrub callback during press/drag/release, and [FilePlayback::setPositionMs](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/FilePlayback.cpp:224) clears the speaker FIFO and resets/seeks the decoder every time. That can create a small audible gap/drop around the scrub action.

One caveat: encrypted MP3 playback borrows `AudioFileRecorder`’s static decrypt buffers in [Mp3EncryptedPlayback.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/Mp3EncryptedPlayback.cpp:147). If you are playing/scrubbing encrypted audio while an encrypted recording is active, those shared buffers look uncoordinated and could plausibly cause a one-chunk hiccup. If you are only playing back, I’d blame scrub-time FIFO/decode reset, not Shine.
I checked it read-only. Short version: your suspicion is half right, but probably not the cause of the observed behavior.

`disable_bit_reservoir` is accepted by [ShineWrapper.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/ShineWrapper.cpp:17) and stored as `disable_bit_reservoir_`, but I found no code that actually uses that member. So as an option, it is not respected.
But the vendored Shine encoder appears to have the reservoir effectively disabled already:
- [reservoir.c](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/shine/src/lib/reservoir.c:30) immediately returns normal per-frame bits when `ResvMax` is zero.
- [l3bitstream.c](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ShineWrapper/shine/src/lib/l3bitstream.c:103) writes `main_data_begin` as zero for both MPEG-I and MPEG-II paths.
So the flag is a no-op, but the actual output should still be no-reservoir. Since your recorder is 16 kHz / 64 kbps MPEG-II Layer III, frames should be standalone enough for frame-aligned scrubbing.

The “lose one packet while locally playing back while scrubbing” sounds more like playback mechanics than encoder reservoir. `ScrubBar` calls the scrub callback during press/drag/release, and [FilePlayback::setPositionMs](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/FilePlayback.cpp:224) clears the speaker FIFO and resets/seeks the decoder every time. That can create a small audible gap/drop around the scrub action.

One caveat: encrypted MP3 playback borrows `AudioFileRecorder`’s static decrypt buffers in [Mp3EncryptedPlayback.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/Mp3EncryptedPlayback.cpp:147). If you are playing/scrubbing encrypted audio while an encrypted recording is active, those shared buffers look uncoordinated and could plausibly cause a one-chunk hiccup. If you are only playing back, I’d blame scrub-time FIFO/decode reset, not Shine.
